claims.normalized_claim: On May 6, 2010, U.S. stock markets experienced the 'Flash Crash': beginning around 2:32 p.m. EDT and lasting about 36 minutes, the Dow Jones Industrial Average fell roughly 998.5 points (about 9%), with about $1 trillion in market value temporarily wiped out before a large part of the loss was recovered the same afternoon.
allowed_wording: On May 6, 2010, the market fell almost a thousand points in minutes and recovered most of it within about half an hour, briefly erasing around a trillion dollars.

claims.normalized_claim: By 2010 U.S. equity and futures markets were dominated by automated, high-frequency trading firms whose algorithms react to the order book in fractions of a second and provide liquidity, but which are not obligated to keep trading and can withdraw.
allowed_wording: The modern market is mostly machines trading with machines in millionths of a second; they provide liquidity but are not required to stay.

claims.normalized_claim: Navinder Singh Sarao traded futures from his parents' house in Hounslow, west London, and was later diagnosed with severe autism (Asperger's syndrome).
allowed_wording: He traded from a bedroom in his parents' house in Hounslow; doctors later diagnosed severe autism.
prohibited_wording: His autism made him a criminal.
claims.notes: Handle the diagnosis with dignity; attribute to the record/specialist (CLM-0011).

claims.normalized_claim: From at least 2009 to 2014, Sarao used a modified, commercially available automated trading program to place and rapidly cancel very large orders in the E-mini S&P 500 futures market on the Chicago Mercantile Exchange.
allowed_wording: He took ordinary trading software and rewrote it to place huge orders and pull them back automatically, on S&P 500 futures.

claims.normalized_claim: Spoofing is placing large orders a trader intends to cancel before execution in order to create a false impression of supply or demand and move the price; Sarao admitted making at least $12.8 million in illicit gains from his scheme, and the CFTC alleged he profited over $40 million from E-mini trading.
allowed_wording: Spoofing means placing orders you mean to cancel, to fool the market about supply and demand; he admitted at least $12.8 million, and regulators alleged he made far more.

claims.normalized_claim: Prosecutors and regulators said that on May 6, 2010, Sarao's spoofing program contributed to the order imbalance that helped tip the market into the Flash Crash, and that he was 'significantly responsible' for those imbalances; no authoritative account holds that he single-handedly caused the crash.
counterevidence: The 2010 joint report initially emphasized a large fundamental sell program, not Sarao (CLM-0007).
allowed_wording: Officials said his orders contributed to the imbalance that helped tip the market over, and that he was significantly responsible for that imbalance.
prohibited_wording: One man broke the stock market (as literal fact).
He single-handedly crashed Wall Street (as fact).
He triggered the crash by himself.
claims.notes: R3 LOAD-BEARING: causation attributed as 'contributed / exacerbated / significantly responsible'. NEVER 'caused / single-handedly'. The narrator does not assert sole cause; attribute to officials and always pair with CLM-0007.

claims.normalized_claim: The 2010 SEC/CFTC joint report identified a large fundamental seller (a mutual-fund firm) that sold 75,000 E-mini S&P 500 contracts (about $4.1 billion) using a price- and time-insensitive algorithm as a key factor in the crash, and a 2014 CFTC study concluded that high-frequency traders did not cause the crash but contributed to it by demanding immediacy.
allowed_wording: The first official report pointed at a $4-billion automated sell order; later studies said the high-frequency machines did not cause the crash but deepened it.
prohibited_wording: The mutual fund deliberately crashed the market.
Name-and-shame a specific firm as the villain.
claims.notes: The multi-factor truth; keep specific firms neutral (lawful sell order). Pair with CLM-0006.

claims.normalized_claim: In 2015 U.S. authorities charged Sarao (wire fraud, commodities fraud, and spoofing); he was arrested in London and fought extradition to the United States.
allowed_wording: In 2015 they charged him and arrested him in London; he fought extradition and lost.

claims.normalized_claim: In 2016 Sarao pleaded guilty to one count of wire fraud and one count of spoofing, and agreed to forfeit about $12.9 million.
allowed_wording: In 2016 he pleaded guilty to wire fraud and spoofing and agreed to give back nearly thirteen million dollars.
prohibited_wording: He pleaded guilty to causing the Flash Crash.
He was convicted of crashing the market.
claims.notes: STATE AS FACT: guilty plea to wire fraud + spoofing. NOT 'guilty of causing the crash'.

claims.normalized_claim: Sarao lost most of the money he had made to fraudulent investment schemes, including a Ponzi scheme, leaving little of his trading fortune.
evidence_locations: Lost over £40m to fraudsters / Ponzi scheme
allowed_wording: Most of the fortune was gone — not spent, but taken: he had handed it to con men and a fraud.
claims.notes: The irony: the man who fooled the market was himself defrauded. Verify exact figure at fact_recheck.

claims.normalized_claim: Sarao cooperated extensively with U.S. authorities; prosecutors sought leniency citing his cooperation and his severe autism, and an autism specialist stated that his lack of interest in the money and his obsessive, game-like trading were consistent with his condition.
evidence_locations: Specialist statement: money meaningless, played like beating a high score
allowed_wording: He cooperated fully; prosecutors asked for mercy, pointing to his autism — an expert said the money meant nothing to him and he traded like a child beating a high score.
claims.notes: Attribute the characterization to the specialist/record; dignity, not spectacle.

claims.normalized_claim: In January 2020 a U.S. federal judge sentenced Sarao to time served plus one year of home incarceration, with no further prison time, despite his having faced the possibility of years in prison.
evidence_locations: 2020 sentence: time served + 1 year home incarceration
allowed_wording: In 2020 the judge gave him no further prison — one year at home — though he had faced years behind bars.
claims.notes: STATE AS FACT: home incarceration, no further prison. Verify exact terms at fact_recheck.

claims.normalized_claim: The Flash Crash had no single cause: it resulted from the interaction of a large automated sell program, high-frequency traders withdrawing liquidity, a nervous market, and Sarao's spoofing — so attributing the entire crash to one person is not supported by the record.
evidence_locations: Multi-factor causation across the official record
allowed_wording: No serious account says one man, alone, broke the market that day — a careless sell order, self-protecting machines, a frightened world, and a ghost in the order book, all at once.
prohibited_wording: A single trader caused the crash.
The mystery is solved: it was one man.
claims.notes: R3 CRITICAL nuance for fairness; this is the thesis guardrail against 'one man broke the market' as literal fact.

findings: Durable source hashes pending (content_hash=sha256:pending) -> compute at fact_recheck before publish. R2/R3: living person (Sarao) -> pre-publish legal/rights review required. Causation locked: contributed/exacerbated/significantly responsible ALLOWED; caused/single-handedly PROHIBITED (CLM-0006/0013). Guilty plea (wire fraud + spoofing) = fact (CLM-0009).
